message:
fcp-core/policy: content-address sanitizer_receipt_object_id to prevent receipt_id collision

`sanitizer_receipt_object_id` was deriving the audit-evidence ObjectId
from `receipt.receipt_id` alone:

    fn sanitizer_receipt_object_id(receipt: &SanitizerReceipt) -> ObjectId {
        ObjectId::from_unscoped_bytes(receipt.receipt_id.as_bytes())
    }

The `SanitizerReceipt` struct (`crates/fcp-core/src/provenance.rs:915`)
documents `receipt_id` as "Unique receipt ID (becomes ObjectId when
stored)". That contract is caller-side only — nothing in fcp-core
enforces uniqueness. An issuer who mints two receipts with the same
`receipt_id` but different `covered_inputs`, `sanitizer_zone`,
`cleared_flags`, or `signature` produces collision-bound ObjectIds.

Where the collision lands today:

* `apply_sanitizer_receipts` (`crates/fcp-core/src/policy.rs:3023`)
  computes the id and feeds it into both
  `provenance.apply_taint_reduction(...)` and the policy decision's
  `evidence: Vec<ObjectId>`.
* `apply_taint_reduction` (`crates/fcp-core/src/provenance.rs:657`)
  builds a `TaintReduction { sanitizer_receipt_id, ... }` and pushes
  it onto `taint_reductions: Vec<TaintReduction>`.
* The reduction record is the audit reference for "this taint flag was
  cleared by THIS receipt". With colliding ObjectIds, two distinct
  receipts can clear the same flag with the same audit reference —
  post-hoc lookup cannot tell which of R1 or R2 was applied. That is
  the confused-deputy class: an auditor walking back from
  `TaintReduction.sanitizer_receipt_id` to "the receipt that
  authorized this clearance" gets an ambiguous answer.

Where the collision will land tomorrow: the `receipt_id` doc says
"becomes ObjectId when stored". `Grep` confirms no
`HashMap<ObjectId, SanitizerReceipt>` or analogous content-addressed
store exists in fcp-store, fcp-host, or anywhere else. The moment
that store ships with the old derivation, two receipts with the same
`receipt_id` overwrite each other on insert — actual data loss.

Fix mirrors the same primary/fallback split already used by
`approval_token_object_id` (committed in 6d273157, refactored in
dd367aba) so the two evidence types follow one rule:

1. Primary: `fcp_cbor::to_canonical_cbor(receipt)` — the stable wire
   encoding, deterministic, includes every field. Every byte goes
   through `from_unscoped_bytes` so the resulting ObjectId is bound
   to the full content.
2. Fallback (only triggers on canonical-CBOR failure, e.g. an
   adversary-supplied signature pushing the receipt past
   `MAX_CANONICAL_OBJECT_BYTES`): length-delimited framed hash that
   mixes every field. Domain separator
   `b"FCP/SanitizerReceipt/fallback/v1"` keeps the fallback id space
   distinct from the canonical one. Trailing
   `u8::from(signature.is_some())` distinguishes `None` from
   `Some(empty)`.

This intentionally matches the ApprovalToken story for symmetry — the
audit told that asymmetry was the next foot-gun once a receipt store
landed; landing this change now closes the gap before any consumer
relies on the old behavior.

Regression test
`test_sanitizer_receipt_object_id_collision_resistance`:

* Builds a baseline receipt with `receipt_id = "rcpt-collision"`.
* Mutates only `covered_inputs` → asserts the ObjectId changes.
  Pre-fix this would fail; post-fix it passes.
* Mutates only `sanitizer_zone` → asserts the ObjectId changes.
* Adds a `signature: Some(64-byte vec)` → asserts the ObjectId
  changes (the standard "issuer re-signed an existing receipt id"
  attack surface).
* Asserts `id_base != ObjectId::from_unscoped_bytes(b"rcpt-collision")`
  — the previous derivation would have produced exactly that value;
  the new derivation must not.
* Asserts determinism: two calls on the same receipt produce
  identical ObjectIds.

Compatibility: `sanitizer_receipt_object_id` is a private fn, callable
only from `apply_sanitizer_receipts` (same file, line 3023). The
ObjectId it produces feeds two `Vec::push` paths
(`taint_reductions` and `PolicyDecision.evidence`), neither of which
key on the value, neither of which has a dedup invariant that depends
on the prior derivation. No public API changes; no test fixtures
under `crates/fcp-core/tests/` assert on the specific ObjectId bytes
this function produced.

Verified locally with rch CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/fcp-cc1:
* `cargo test -p fcp-core --lib` → 3913 passed, 0 failed
* `cargo test -p fcp-core --tests` → 47 integration passed
* `cargo clippy -p fcp-core --lib -- -D warnings` → clean

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
